summary: Cathy O'Neil reports on the successful completion of the first half of the Lede Program, a data journalism training initiative. The students presented impressive projects and learned valuable lessons about data work, particularly around data cleaning and drilling down to find stories. The second half will involve more advanced topics including geocoding, algorithms, and prerequisite math concepts. O'Neil is enthusiastic about the students' progress and their readiness for more complex data journalism projects.
key_points: Students completed their first data projects and presented them successfully
Key learning: data cleaning takes most of the time, and finding the story requires drilling down into data
Journalists are motivated by deadlines and portfolio-building opportunities
The program will cover algorithms, geocoding, and math prerequisites like linear algebra and statistics
O'Neil is using accessible statistics books including 'How to Lie with Statistics' and 'Statistics Done Wrong'
money_quotes: I spent most of my time cleaning my data
Next time I will spend more time thinking about how to drill down in my data to find an interesting story
These are key phrases for people learning lessons with data
Since they are journalists they love projects because they love deadlines and they want something they can add to their portfolio
They'll be well equipped to do some seriously cool shit for their final project
key_insight: Learning to work with data means discovering that most of your time goes to cleaning it and that finding a compelling story requires thoughtful exploration rather than surface-level analysis.
